whatIs.title: What is an Earthquake?
whatIs.intro: An earthquake is the sudden shaking of the Earth's surface caused by the release of energy in the Earth's crust.

plates.title: Tectonic Plates
plates.desc: The Earth's outer shell is divided into several plates that glide over the mantle.

faults.title: Fault Lines
faults.desc: A fault is a fracture in the Earth's crust where blocks of rock have moved relative to each other.

waves.title: Seismic Waves
waves.desc: Energy from earthquakes travels through the Earth as seismic waves.

measure.title: How to Measure an Earthquake?
measure.intro: Earthquakes are measured using instruments called seismometers. The two main scales used are the Richter Scale and the Moment Magnitude Scale.

richter.title: Richter Scale
richter.p1: Developed by Charles Richter in 1935, this was the first widely used earthquake magnitude scale.
richter.p2: It measures the amplitude of seismic waves recorded by seismometers.

moment.title: Moment Magnitude Scale (Mw)
moment.p1: The modern standard for measuring large earthquakes, developed in 1979.
moment.p2: It measures the total energy released by the earthquake.

measure.energyDesc: A magnitude 9.0 earthquake releases as much energy as 480 million tons of TNT — equivalent to about 31,000 Hiroshima atomic bombs.

micro.label: Micro
micro.desc: Not felt by people. Recorded by seismographs only. Millions occur each year.

minor.label: Minor
minor.desc: Felt by some people indoors. Rarely causes damage. Like a passing truck.

light.label: Light
light.desc: Felt by most people. Dishes rattle, doors swing. Minor damage possible.

moderate.label: Moderate
moderate.desc: Felt by everyone. Furniture moves. Some structural damage to weak buildings.

strong.label: Strong
strong.desc: Damage to well-built structures. Felt over wide areas. Can be destructive in populated regions.

major.label: Major
major.desc: Serious damage over large areas. Buildings collapse. Ground cracks appear.

great.label: Great
great.desc: Severe damage over vast areas. Total destruction near the epicenter. Affects millions.

devastating.label: Devastating
devastating.desc: Catastrophic destruction. Entire regions leveled. Triggers tsunamis and landslides.

safety.title: Earthquake Safety Guide
safety.intro: Remember the three essential steps: Drop, Cover, and Hold On. Stay calm and protect yourself.

step1.title: DROP
step1.desc: Drop to your hands and knees. This position prevents you from being knocked down.

step2.title: COVER
step2.desc: Cover your head and neck with your arms. If possible, get under a sturdy table or desk.
step2.tip: Stay away from windows and heavy objects.

step3.title: HOLD ON
step3.desc: Hold on to your shelter until the shaking stops. Be prepared to move with your shelter.
step3.tip: If no shelter is nearby, cover your head and neck.

step4.title: STAY CALM
step4.desc: After shaking stops, check for injuries and damage. Be prepared for aftershocks.
step4.tip: Aftershocks can be strong — stay alert!

before.title: Before an Earthquake
before.intro: Preparation is key to surviving an earthquake. Take these steps before the ground starts shaking.

sec1.title: Secure Your Space
sec1.desc: Identify safe spots in each room and secure heavy furniture.

sec2.title: Make a Plan
sec2.desc: Create a family emergency plan and practice it regularly.

sec3.title: Build a Kit
sec3.desc: Prepare an emergency supply kit that lasts at least 72 hours.

sec4.title: Know Your Risks
sec4.desc: Understand the earthquake risk in your area and take appropriate precautions.

during.title: During an Earthquake
during.intro: If an earthquake strikes, remember: Drop, Cover, and Hold On. Stay calm and protect yourself.
during.warning: ⚠️ Do NOT run outside during shaking — falling debris is the biggest danger.

after.title: After an Earthquake
after.intro: The shaking has stopped, but dangers remain. Follow these steps to stay safe after an earthquake.
after.avoidTitle: Things to Avoid After an Earthquake
after.recoveryCta: Need help with recovery? Visit our Recovery Resources page for step-by-step guides.

sec1.title: Check for Injuries
sec1.desc: First check yourself for injuries, then help others if you can do so safely.

sec2.title: Check for Hazards
sec2.desc: Look for fire, gas leaks, structural damage, and downed power lines.

sec3.title: Stay Informed
sec3.desc: Listen to official sources for updates and instructions.

sec4.title: Aftershocks
sec4.desc: Be prepared for aftershocks — they can be nearly as strong as the main quake.

sec5.title: Help Others
sec5.desc: If you are able, help neighbors and community members who need assistance.
